</ee:cache>
options: The Cache scope cannot contain database operations
The cache key is built only from the request path, so all category values share one cache entry
keyGenerationExpression must return a number, not a string
The Cache scope never stores payloads produced by SELECT statements because they are streams
optionNotes: Any non-void processor chain can run inside a Cache scope; the database operation is fine.

Correct. attributes.requestPath is '/products' for every category, so the first category queried populates the entry that every other category then receives. The key must include the parts of the request that change the response, e.g. attributes.requestPath ++ (attributes.queryParams.category default '').
Keys can be any expression result; strings are the norm.
Streamed payloads are consumed into a repeatable form by the caching strategy; SELECT results are cacheable.
questions.explanation: A Cache scope key must capture everything that makes a response unique. Keying only on requestPath collapses all query-parameter variants into a single entry, serving whichever category was cached first. Including the category parameter in keyGenerationExpression fixes it.

questions.q: An HTTP-triggered flow sets maxConcurrency="2". Ten requests arrive at the same time and each takes about 5 seconds to process. What happens to the requests that cannot be processed immediately?
options: They are queued indefinitely inside the flow until a thread is available
Back-pressure applies and the HTTP Listener rejects excess requests with 503
They are processed anyway because maxConcurrency only applies to batch jobs
The listener silently drops the connections without a response
optionNotes: Mule 4 does not queue unbounded work at a flow that is saturated; back-pressure is signalled to the source instead.

Correct. When the flow cannot accept more events, the runtime triggers back-pressure (FLOW_BACK_PRESSURE); the HTTP Listener translates that into a 503 Service Unavailable response for requests beyond what the flow can accept.
maxConcurrency applies to flows (and some scopes); it limits how many events the flow processes in parallel.
Clients receive an explicit 503 response, not a dropped connection.
questions.explanation: maxConcurrency caps parallel executions of a flow. When the cap is hit, back-pressure propagates to the message source; the HTTP Listener honours it by answering 503, letting well-behaved clients retry rather than overwhelming the app.

questions.q: A Mule application reads 1 GB files from SFTP and writes them unchanged to Amazon S3. The payload is read exactly once and memory usage must stay minimal. Which streaming strategy is most appropriate for the SFTP read operation?
options: Repeatable in-memory stream
Repeatable file-store stream
Non-repeatable stream
Disable streaming and load the file fully into the payload
optionNotes: Buffers the entire payload in heap — the opposite of minimal memory for 1 GB files.
Works, but it copies the stream to a temporary file so it can be re-read; that disk buffering is wasted effort when the data is consumed exactly once.
Correct. A non-repeatable stream hands the bytes straight through to the single consumer with no buffering at all — the most efficient choice when the payload is read exactly once.
Loading 1 GB into memory defeats the requirement entirely.
questions.explanation: Non-repeatable streaming is the zero-overhead option: bytes flow directly from source to consumer and can be read only once. Repeatable strategies exist to allow multiple reads and cost either heap (in-memory) or disk (file-store).

questions.q: Which two statements about the Parallel For Each scope in Mule 4 are true? (Choose 2.)
options: After the scope completes, the payload is a list of the results of each iteration, in the original collection order
Variables set inside an iteration are visible to the rest of the flow after the scope
Variable changes made inside iterations are discarded when the scope finishes
It processes elements strictly one at a time unless wrapped in an Async scope
optionNotes: True. Parallel For Each aggregates each route's result into a list that preserves the order of the input collection, regardless of completion order.
False — each iteration works on a copy of the event, so its variable changes do not survive the scope.
True. Because iterations run on copies of the event, any variables they set (or modify) are lost after the scope; only the aggregated payload comes out.
False — running iterations concurrently is the entire point of Parallel For Each (concurrency is tunable with maxConcurrency).
questions.explanation: Parallel For Each runs iterations concurrently on copies of the event and returns a list of results in input order. The copy semantics mean per-iteration variable changes are discarded — a key behavioural difference from the sequential For Each, which shares the event and keeps variable changes but leaves the payload unchanged.

questions.q: A Mule application declares the MySQL JDBC driver as shown in the exhibit, yet deployment fails with 'Cannot load class com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver' when the Database connector initializes. Why?
options: The driver must also be a sharedLibrary in the mule-maven-plugin so the connector sees it
MySQL drivers are not supported on Mule 4 and must be replaced with a generic JDBC bridge
The version is incompatible; only 5.x drivers work with the Database connector
The dependency needs <scope>provided</scope> so the runtime supplies the class
optionNotes: Correct. Mule 4 isolates classloaders: a plain dependency is visible only to the application, not to plugins such as the Database connector. Declaring it under mule-maven-plugin > classloaderModelLoaderDescriptor sharedLibraries (groupId/artifactId) exports it to the plugins' classloaders.
MySQL is fully supported; the failure is a classloading issue, not a compatibility one.
Driver 8.x works fine once shared; the version is not the problem.
provided scope would remove the JAR from the deployable archive entirely — the runtime does not bundle JDBC drivers.
questions.explanation: Because of Mule 4's classloading isolation, JDBC drivers (and similar libraries used by connectors) must be listed in the mule-maven-plugin's <sharedLibraries> section. That exports them from the application classloader to connector classloaders; a plain <dependency> alone leaves the Database connector unable to load the driver class.

questions.q: A team wants several on-premises Mule applications to share one HTTP Listener configuration and one TLS context by placing them in a Mule domain project. Which limitation must they keep in mind?
options: Domains can share flows and sub-flows but not connector configurations
Domain projects are supported only on customer-hosted Mule runtimes, not on CloudHub
Each application may reference at most one resource from the domain
Domains require every application to use exactly the same Mule runtime patch version as the domain
optionNotes: It is the other way round: domains exist to share resources such as connector configs and TLS contexts; they do not contain flows.

Correct. CloudHub deploys each application in its own isolated worker with no domain support, so shared-resource domains are a customer-hosted (standalone/RTF-excluded) runtime feature. Apps bound to a domain cannot be deployed to CloudHub.
There is no such limit — an app can reference any number of domain resources.
Apps and domain must run in the same runtime instance and be version compatible, but an exact patch-level match is not the defining limitation here.
questions.explanation: Mule domains let multiple apps deployed to the same customer-hosted runtime share connector configurations (HTTP listeners, TLS contexts, DB configs...). Because CloudHub isolates every application in its own worker, domain projects cannot be used there — a common architecture gotcha.

questions.q: A RAML API published to Exchange at version 2.3.1 gets a change that renames a required field in a response type. According to semantic versioning, what should the next published version be?
options: 2.3.1-breaking
optionNotes: A patch bump signals a backwards-compatible fix; renaming a required field breaks existing consumers.
A minor bump signals added, backwards-compatible functionality — not the case here.
Correct. Renaming a required field is a breaking change for every consumer parsing the response, so the major version must be incremented: 3.0.0.
Pre-release labels do not communicate breakage; consumers resolving 2.x would still break.
questions.explanation: SemVer: MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for backwards-compatible additions, PATCH for fixes. A renamed required field breaks consumers, so 2.3.1 → 3.0.0.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does the mule-plugin classifier on this dependency indicate?
options: A reusable Mule extension/plugin that gets its own isolated classloader
The dependency is only used at build time and is excluded from the deployable archive
The artifact is a Mule domain that this application inherits resources from
The dependency will be downloaded from Anypoint Exchange instead of Maven Central
optionNotes: Correct. mule-plugin marks the artifact as a Mule 4 plugin (for example a packaged connector or a common library built as a plugin). The runtime deploys it with its own classloader following Mule's classloading isolation model.
Build-time-only dependencies use scope provided/test; the classifier does not change packaging inclusion that way.
Domains are referenced with the mule-domain classifier and a special parent mechanism, not mule-plugin.
Where it is downloaded from depends on the configured repositories, not on the classifier.
questions.explanation: The mule-plugin classifier tells Maven and the Mule runtime that the artifact is a Mule plugin. Plugins deploy alongside the app with isolated classloaders — the packaging mechanism behind connectors and reusable Mule libraries.

questions.q: A CI/CD pipeline deploys a Mule application to CloudHub 2.0 with the mule-maven-plugin. Where should the Connected App credentials used by the plugin be kept?
options: Hard-coded in the cloudhub2Deployment section of pom.xml so builds are reproducible
In the developer's mule-artifact.json
Outside the project — CI/CD secrets or settings.xml — injected at build time
In a properties file committed next to the pom for the whole team to reuse
optionNotes: pom.xml is committed to source control; credentials in it leak to everyone with repo access and to build logs.
mule-artifact.json describes the application artifact; it is packaged and distributed with the app — the worst place for secrets.

Correct. The plugin resolves ${...} properties, so credentials belong in CI/CD secret stores (or an encrypted settings.xml server entry) and are passed to the build as properties/environment variables, never committed.
Committed properties files have the same exposure problem as the pom itself.
questions.explanation: Deployment credentials must stay out of version control. The standard pattern is to reference them as Maven properties in the deployment configuration and supply the values from the CI/CD platform's secret management (or ~/.m2/settings.xml with encryption) at build time.

questions.q: A team builds a common DataWeave library (reusable .dwl modules) that several Mule applications should import. What is the recommended way to distribute and consume it?
options: Copy the .dwl files into each project's src/main/resources folder
Publish it to Anypoint Exchange as a reusable asset and add it as a Maven dependency in each application
Paste the functions into each transformation that needs them
Host the files on a shared network drive referenced by absolute path in the transforms
optionNotes: Copies drift immediately — a bug fix must be re-copied into every project by hand.

Correct. Packaging the library as an asset in Exchange (deployed to the organization's Maven facade) gives every application a versioned <dependency>; upgrades are explicit version bumps and the single source of truth lives in one place.
Inline duplication is the least maintainable option of all.
Absolute paths break across machines and environments and provide no versioning.
questions.explanation: Exchange doubles as a Maven repository for an organization. Publishing shared DataWeave modules (or any reusable library) there and consuming them as versioned Maven dependencies is the MuleSoft-recommended reuse pattern — one source of truth, explicit versioning, CI-friendly.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. The application starts locally with the command 'mvn mule:run' and fails with: 'Could not resolve placeholder secure::db.password'. What is the most likely cause?
questions.exhibit:
<secure-properties:config name="Secure_Props"
    file="config/secure-${env}.yaml" key="${mule.key}">
  <secure-properties:encrypt algorithm="AES" mode="CBC"/>
</secure-properties:config>

<!-- config/secure-dev.yaml -->
db:
  password: "![k1lFactpJ+4wJb2rcvUkMg==]"
options: The mule.key (and/or env) system property was not passed to the runtime at startup
Encrypted values must use the format ${aes:...} instead of ![...]
The Secure Properties module cannot read YAML files
secure:: placeholders only resolve on CloudHub, not locally
optionNotes: Correct. The config derives the file name from ${env} and the decryption key from ${mule.key}; if these are not supplied (e.g. -Denv=dev -Dmule.key=<key>, or -M-D... when running in Studio/standalone), the secure properties provider cannot initialize and no secure:: placeholder resolves.
![...] is exactly the wrapper the Secure Properties module expects for encrypted values.
The module reads .yaml and .properties files alike.
Secure properties work identically on any runtime, local or cloud.
questions.explanation: Secure Properties configs typically parameterize both the environment file and the encryption key. The decryption key is deliberately never packaged with the app — it must arrive as a startup property (mule.key). Forgetting to pass it (or the env selector) is the classic cause of 'Could not resolve placeholder secure::…'.

questions.q: Why must the encryption key used by the Secure Properties module be supplied as a runtime argument instead of being stored in a properties file inside the application?
options: Property files cannot hold values longer than 16 characters
Bundling the key with the app would let anyone with the artifact decrypt every secret it protects
The Mule runtime caches property files, so the key would be stale after rotation
Keys in property files are automatically re-encrypted on deployment, corrupting them
optionNotes: There is no such length limit.

Correct. The entire point of encrypting properties is defeated if the decryption key travels inside the same artifact (or the same repository). Supplying it out-of-band — startup argument, CI/CD secret, platform property marked hidden — keeps the artifact safe to share.
Caching is not the security rationale here.
No such re-encryption mechanism exists.
questions.explanation: Encrypted properties are only as safe as the separation between ciphertext and key. The key therefore lives outside the deployable artifact and source control, passed at startup (e.g. -M-Dmule.key=...) or via secured platform properties.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. An HTTP Request to a partner API fails during the TLS handshake with 'Received fatal alert: certificate_required'. The partner confirms their endpoint enforces mutual TLS. Which change fixes the configuration?
options: Add a tls:key-store with the app's client cert and key
Replace the trust store with the partner's private key
Set insecure="true" on the trust store to skip validation
Change the trust store type from JKS to PKCS12
optionNotes: Correct. In mutual TLS the client must present its own certificate. The trust store only validates the server's certificate; presenting a client certificate requires a tls:key-store entry holding this app's certificate and private key.
Private keys are never exchanged between parties; the partner keeps theirs, we keep ours.
insecure=true disables server-certificate validation on our side — it does nothing about the server requiring a certificate from us, so the handshake still fails.
The store format is irrelevant to the missing-client-certificate problem.
questions.explanation: TLS context roles: trust-store = certificates we trust when validating the other side; key-store = our own identity (certificate + private key) presented to the other side. 'certificate_required' from the server means it wants our client certificate — only a key-store provides that.

questions.q: Deployment properties such as db.password are set in Runtime Manager for a CloudHub application. The team wants these values masked in the Runtime Manager UI and encrypted at rest by the platform. How is that achieved?
options: List the property names in the secureProperties array of mule-artifact.json
Prefix each property value with 'secure::' when typing it in Runtime Manager
Wrap each value in ![...] in the Runtime Manager properties tab
Enable 'Persistent queues' on the application settings page
optionNotes: Correct. Properties named in mule-artifact.json's "secureProperties" array become safely hidden application properties: Runtime Manager stores them encrypted and displays them masked, and they cannot be read back through the UI or API.
secure:: is the prefix used inside the app to resolve properties through the Secure Properties module — it has no effect on how Runtime Manager stores what you type.
![...] marks values encrypted by the Secure Properties tool inside configuration files; Runtime Manager does not interpret it.
Persistent queues concern reliable messaging between workers, not property security.
questions.explanation: Declaring names in the secureProperties array of mule-artifact.json makes those deployment properties 'safely hidden': the platform encrypts them at rest, masks them in the UI, and never returns their values via API — complementary to (and independent of) the in-app Secure Properties module.

questions.q: An integration must store customers' national ID numbers in a database column that auditors may never see in plain text, while the rest of the record stays queryable. Which approach fits best inside the Mule flow?
options: Encrypt only the ID field via the Cryptography module
Enable TLS on the database connection
Store the IDs in a secure properties file instead of the database
Base64-encode the ID field before inserting it
optionNotes: Correct. The Cryptography module (JCE/PGP/XML operations) encrypts message data — here a single field via a DataWeave step plus jce:encrypt (or encrypt-pbe) — so the column holds ciphertext while other columns remain usable.
TLS protects data in transit between app and database; the column itself would still store plain text.
Secure properties protect configuration values of the app, not per-record business data.
Base64 is an encoding anyone can reverse; it provides zero confidentiality.
questions.explanation: Field-level protection of business data is the Cryptography module's job (distinct from the Secure Properties module, which guards configuration). Encrypting the sensitive field before persistence gives at-rest confidentiality without affecting the rest of the record; TLS only ever covers transit.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What is the effect — and the trade-off — of configuring the application's loggers this way?
options: Logging returns immediately; a background thread writes; a crash can lose recent entries
Log entries are written twice, once per logger, doubling disk usage
Asynchronous loggers only work with the JSON layout
The runtime ignores AsyncLogger elements entirely unless Anypoint Monitoring is enabled first
optionNotes: Correct. Async loggers enqueue events to a ring buffer consumed by a separate thread, removing I/O from the processing path — significantly better throughput and latency. The cost: buffered entries that have not been flushed are lost on an abrupt JVM termination.
Each logger covers a different package; nothing is duplicated.
Async logging is independent of the layout used by the appender.
AsyncLogger is plain Log4j 2 configuration honoured by the runtime regardless of monitoring products.
questions.explanation: Log4j 2 async loggers decouple application threads from disk I/O via a lock-free ring buffer — the standard performance tuning for chatty Mule apps. The accepted trade-off is potential loss of the last moments of logging in a hard crash, which is why some teams keep ERROR-level loggers synchronous.

questions.q: Support engineers must trace a single customer transaction across three Mule applications that call each other over HTTP. Logs from all applications are aggregated in Anypoint Monitoring. What makes this end-to-end tracing work with the least custom code?
options: Rely on the correlation ID Mule propagates on outbound HTTP calls and log it everywhere
Add a random UUID variable in each application and log it
Log each application's full payload and match records by comparing their contents
Enable verbose HTTP wire logging in all three applications
optionNotes: Correct. Every Mule event carries a correlation ID, and Mule connectors propagate it automatically on outbound requests (X-Correlation-ID header) so downstream apps adopt the same ID. Logging it (it is part of standard log context / #[correlationId]) lets one search return the transaction's entries from all three apps.
Independent UUIDs per application cannot be joined across the chain — the ID must travel with the request.
Payload matching is fragile, expensive, and risks logging sensitive data.
Wire logs are for debugging connectivity; they are enormous and still would not link the applications' entries reliably.
questions.explanation: Mule's built-in correlation ID is designed exactly for this: it is generated at the edge, propagated automatically across Mule-to-Mule HTTP hops, and available in logs and expressions. Searching the aggregated logs by that one ID reconstructs the full journey.

questions.q: The operations team wants an email whenever a production CloudHub application's worker exceeds 80% memory usage for five minutes. Where is this configured?
options: An alert in Runtime Manager / Anypoint Monitoring on the app's memory metric
In the application's log4j2.xml
In a scheduled MUnit test that checks memory
In the mule-artifact.json minMuleVersion field of the deployed application
optionNotes: Correct. Runtime Manager (and Anypoint Monitoring for richer conditions) provides alerts on infrastructure metrics — CPU, memory, response time, application events — with thresholds, durations, and email/webhook notifications, no code changes needed.
log4j2.xml shapes what the app logs; it cannot watch worker resource usage or send threshold emails.
MUnit runs at build time against test cases, not against production workers.
minMuleVersion declares runtime compatibility; it is unrelated to monitoring.
questions.explanation: Resource-threshold notifications are a platform feature: define an alert on the application in Runtime Manager/Anypoint Monitoring with the metric (memory), threshold (80%), duration (5 min) and recipients. Applications need no changes.

questions.q: A Mule API depends on a database and a downstream SOAP service. The platform team wants a health endpoint that load balancers can poll to take an unhealthy instance out of rotation. Which implementation is most appropriate?
options: A GET /health flow that checks each dependency with short timeouts, returning 200 only if all are up
Return 200 from /health unconditionally, since the app being up is what matters most
Expose the Mule runtime's JMX port directly to the load balancer
Schedule a flow that emails operations when a dependency is down
optionNotes: Correct. A health-check flow that probes critical dependencies (e.g. SELECT 1, lightweight ping operation) with aggressive timeouts and maps the results to 200/503 gives the load balancer a truthful signal, and the short timeouts keep the endpoint itself fast.
An unconditional 200 reports a zombie instance as healthy — traffic keeps flowing to an app that cannot serve it.
JMX is neither an HTTP health protocol nor safe to expose; load balancers expect a simple HTTP probe.
Emails alert humans; they do not let the load balancer react automatically.
questions.explanation: A useful health endpoint verifies what the application needs to do its job, not merely that HTTP is listening. Checking each dependency with short timeouts and returning 200/503 accordingly integrates cleanly with load balancer health probes and keeps failure detection automatic.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does this MUnit configuration accomplish?
questions.exhibit:
<munit-tools:mock-when processor="http:request">
  <munit-tools:with-attributes>
    <munit-tools:with-attribute attributeName="doc:name" whereValue="Get customer"/>
  </munit-tools:with-attributes>
  <munit-tools:then-return>
    <munit-tools:payload value='#[{"id": 42, "status": "ACTIVE"}]' mediaType="application/json"/>
  </munit-tools:then-return>
</munit-tools:mock-when>
options: The HTTP Request named 'Get customer' isn't executed; the mock returns the given JSON instead
It asserts that the HTTP Request returns exactly that JSON in production
It rewrites the flow permanently to return static data
It mocks every processor in the flow at once, not only the named HTTP Request
optionNotes: Correct. mock-when intercepts matching processors during the MUnit test run. The with-attribute filter narrows the match to the operation whose doc:name is 'Get customer', which then returns the stubbed payload — isolating the flow under test from the real API.
Assertions are made with assert-that / verify-call; mock-when substitutes behaviour, it does not assert.
Mocks exist only inside the test execution; the deployed application is untouched.
The processor attribute plus the doc:name filter target one specific operation.
questions.explanation: mock-when is MUnit's stubbing tool: match a processor (optionally filtered by attributes such as doc:name) and substitute its result. Tests thus run without external dependencies, deterministic and fast.

questions.q: A flow raises a custom APP:ORDER_NOT_FOUND error through a Raise Error component when a lookup misses. Which MUnit approach verifies that behaviour directly?
options: Set expectedErrorType="APP:ORDER_NOT_FOUND" on the munit:test
Wrap the flow-ref in a Try scope inside the test and assert the payload afterwards
Use mock-when on the Raise Error component so it does not fire
Check the application log for the error message with a file assertion
optionNotes: Correct. munit:test supports expectedErrorType (and expectedException/expectedErrorDescription): the test executes the flow and passes precisely when the declared error type propagates — the idiomatic way to test error paths.
Possible but roundabout: it forces manual error-to-payload plumbing that expectedErrorType provides declaratively.
Mocking the Raise Error away tests the opposite of the requirement — the error is what must be verified.
Log scraping is brittle and does not prove the error type actually propagated to the caller.
questions.explanation: For negative-path tests, declare the expected error type on the test itself: <munit:test ... expectedErrorType="APP:ORDER_NOT_FOUND">. The test fails if the flow completes normally or raises a different type.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What is the effect of this configuration on the Maven build?
options: MUnit fails the build below 80% application coverage
The build fails if more than 80% of tests fail
Coverage is reported but can never fail the build
Only 80% of the MUnit tests are executed to save time
optionNotes: Correct. runCoverage enables the coverage report, requiredApplicationCoverage sets the threshold (percentage of message processors exercised by tests), and failBuild=true turns a shortfall into a build failure — a common CI quality gate.
The threshold applies to coverage of the application's processors, not to the test pass rate (any failing test already fails the build).
failBuild=true is exactly what makes the threshold enforcing.
All tests always run; coverage measures what they exercise.
questions.explanation: The munit-maven-plugin coverage block turns test coverage into a CI gate: with failBuild and requiredApplicationCoverage set, a build whose MUnit tests exercise less than the required percentage of the application fails, keeping untested code out of production.

questions.q: A transactional Try scope wraps a JMS consume and a DB insert. The DB insert fails. Given the error handler shown, what happens to the JMS message and the DB write?
questions.exhibit:
<try transactionalAction="ALWAYS_BEGIN">
  <jms:consume config-ref="JMS" destination="orders"/>
  <db:insert config-ref="DB"> ... </db:insert>   <!-- fails -->
  <error-handler>
    <on-error-continue type="DB:CONNECTIVITY">
      <logger message="swallowed"/>
    </on-error-continue>
  </error-handler>
</try>
options: Both roll back: the message returns to the queue and the DB write is undone
The transaction commits: the message is acked and the failed DB write is simply lost
Only the DB write rolls back; the JMS message is redelivered anyway
The runtime throws because JMS cannot join a Try-scope transaction
questions.explanation: On Error Continue marks the processing successful, so the transaction commits — the JMS message is acknowledged even though the DB insert failed and produced nothing. Use On Error Propagate to roll back and let the message redeliver.
optionNotes: Wrong — rollback needs On Error Propagate; Continue commits.
Correct — Continue commits, acking the message and losing the DB write.
Wrong — a single transaction commits or rolls back as a unit; you can't split it here.
Wrong — JMS is transactional and joins the Try-scope transaction fine.

questions.q: An HTTP-triggered flow is built as shown for reliable acquisition. If the app crashes right after the HTTP 202 is returned but before processing finishes, what is the outcome?
questions.exhibit:
<flow name="receive">
  <http:listener path="/orders"/>
  <vm:publish config-ref="VM" queueName="orders" queueType="PERSISTENT"/>
  <set-payload value='#[{accepted: true}]' />   <!-- 202 to caller -->
</flow>
<flow name="process">
  <vm:listener config-ref="VM" queueName="orders" transactionalAction="ALWAYS_BEGIN"/>
  ... processing ...
</flow>
options: The accepted order is lost because the response was already sent
The order survives in the persistent queue and is processed after restart
The caller receives an HTTP 500 because processing never completed
The order is processed twice, once per flow, on restart
questions.explanation: Reliable acquisition persists the message (persistent VM queue) before acknowledging, then processes it in a separate transactional flow. A crash after the ack doesn't lose the order — it stays queued and is processed once the app restarts.
optionNotes: Wrong — persisting before ack is exactly what prevents the loss.
Correct — the persistent queue holds the order until it's processed after restart.
Wrong — the caller already got its 202; the crash doesn't change that response.
Wrong — the transactional consumer processes each message once, not twice.

questions.q: The secure configuration shown is used, and the app is started with -M-Dmule.key=s3cret. How must db.password be referenced elsewhere in the config?
questions.exhibit:
<secure-properties:config name="Secure"
    file="config/secure-${env}.yaml" key="${mule.key}">
  <secure-properties:encrypt algorithm="AES" mode="CBC"/>
</secure-properties:config>

# config/secure-dev.yaml
db:
  password: "![k1lFactpJ+4wJb2rcvUkMg==]"
options: As ${db.password}, the same as any ordinary property
As ${secure::db.password}, using the mandatory secure:: prefix
As #[secure(db.password)] inside a DataWeave expression
As ${encrypted:db.password} to trigger decryption
questions.explanation: Every property in a secure-properties file is referenced with the mandatory secure:: prefix — ${secure::db.password} — even properties that aren't encrypted. The value is decrypted at runtime using the key supplied by ${mule.key}.
optionNotes: Wrong — a plain ${db.password} won't resolve for a secure-file property.
Correct — the secure:: prefix is mandatory for secure-file properties.
Wrong — there's no secure() DataWeave function for this; it's a property placeholder.
Wrong — the prefix is secure::, not encrypted:.

questions.q: Given this log4j2.xml, where will the Logger output for category com.acme.orders at level DEBUG go, and will it appear?
options: It appears in the file, because the root appender captures everything
It is suppressed, because the com.acme.orders category is set to INFO
It appears only if Anypoint Monitoring is enabled for the app
It causes a startup error because DEBUG is not a valid level
questions.explanation: A DEBUG message is below the category's configured INFO level, so it is filtered out before any appender is consulted. Raise com.acme.orders to DEBUG (typically only in dev) to see it.
optionNotes: Wrong — level filtering happens before the appender; the entry never reaches the file.
Correct — INFO on that category suppresses DEBUG/TRACE.
Wrong — logging levels are independent of Anypoint Monitoring.
Wrong — DEBUG is a valid Log4j level; nothing errors.

questions.q: An MUnit test contains the components shown. What does the test actually verify?
questions.exhibit:
<munit:test name="placeOrder-test">
  <munit:execution>
    <flow-ref name="placeOrder"/>
  </munit:execution>
  <munit:validation>
    <munit-tools:verify-call processor="flow-ref" times="2">
      <munit-tools:with-attributes>
        <munit-tools:with-attribute attributeName="name" whereValue="notify"/>
      </munit-tools:with-attributes>
    </munit-tools:verify-call>
  </munit:validation>
</munit:test>
options: That the placeOrder flow returns a payload equal to 2
That the 'notify' flow-ref was invoked exactly twice during the run
That the 'notify' flow is mocked to run only twice
That the test retries the placeOrder flow twice on failure
questions.explanation: Verify Call asserts an invocation count for a processor matched by attributes — here the flow-ref named 'notify' must have been called exactly twice. It doesn't replace behaviour (that's Mock When) or check payload values (that's Assert That).
optionNotes: Wrong — payload equality is Assert That, not Verify Call.
Correct — Verify Call with times=2 asserts the 'notify' call count.
Wrong — Verify Call asserts counts; it doesn't mock or limit execution.
Wrong — MUnit doesn't retry the flow; Verify Call only counts invocations.

sections.title: Building Performant and Reliable Mule Applications
objectives: Use streaming (repeatable and non-repeatable) to process large payloads
Tune streaming strategies: in-memory vs file-stored buffers
Implement reliability patterns: Until Successful, redelivery policies, dead-letter queues
Use VM queues and Anypoint MQ for asynchronous, reliable processing
Manage transactions (local and XA) and transactional scopes
Use the Cache scope and idempotency (Idempotent Message Validator)
Understand thread pools, back-pressure, and horizontal/vertical scaling
Design for high availability: clustering and multiple workers
sections.notes:
Streaming in Mule 4

Components that return InputStream or streamable collections (File, FTP, Database, HTTP, Sockets, Salesforce…) stream by default, and Mule wraps them in repeatable streams so multiple components can read the same content.

StrategyHow it worksDefaultsTrade-offs
Repeatable file-stored (EE default)Buffers in memory up to a threshold, then overflows to a temp file on disk512 KB in-memory buffer (inMemorySize)Handles any size; disk I/O cost beyond the buffer
Repeatable in-memory (CE default)Buffers in memory, expanding as needed up to a maxinitial 512 KB, +512 KB increments (initialBufferSize, bufferSizeIncrement, maxInMemorySize)Fastest; raises STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED if max is hit
Non-repeatableNo buffering — the raw stream, readable ONCE—Lowest memory/latency; a second read fails or gets an empty/consumed stream
<file:read path="huge.csv">
  <repeatable-file-store-stream inMemorySize="1" bufferUnit="MB"/>
</file:read>

<http:request ...>
  <non-repeatable-stream/>   <!-- read-once optimization -->
</http:request>
  • Streamed objects (auto-paging connectors like Database/Salesforce) buffer by instance count, not bytes: in-memory default 500 objects (+100 increments); the EE file-store variant serializes overflow with Kryo.
  • DataWeave: reader property streaming=true processes input sequentially (CSV/JSON — access data in order, single pass); writer property deferred=true streams output downstream before the whole result is materialized.
  • Gotchas: logging #[payload], the debugger's payload preview, or multiple concurrent reads of a non-repeatable stream consume it. Repeatable streams solve this at the cost of buffering.

Reliability patterns

  • Reliable acquisition flow: couple a non-transactional source (HTTP) with a transactional/persistent channel: receive → write to a persistent VM queue (or JMS/Anypoint MQ) → acknowledge the caller. A separate application logic flow consumes from the queue with transactionalAction="ALWAYS_BEGIN" — if processing fails, the message rolls back to the queue instead of being lost.
  • VM queues: intra-app (or intra-cluster) messaging. queueType="PERSISTENT" survives restarts using file-based storage (standalone runtimes) — no extra software; in-memory (TRANSIENT) is faster but lost on crash. Publish with sendCorrelationId="ALWAYS" to keep end-to-end tracing.
  • Until Successful: wraps processors and retries them until success, up to maxRetries with millisBetweenRetries; exhaustion raises MULE:RETRY_EXHAUSTED. For synchronous in-flow retries (flaky API call).
  • Reconnection strategies (on connector configs): retry the connection (count/frequency or forever) — distinct from retrying the operation.
  • Redelivery policy (on listeners): counts how many times the same message fails processing; exceeding maxRedeliveryCount raises MULE:REDELIVERY_EXHAUSTED — handle it by parking the message in a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for inspection/replay.
  • Anypoint MQ (hosted cloud messaging): standard queues, FIFO queues (ordering), message exchanges (fan-out pub/sub), ack modes (IMMEDIATE / AUTO / MANUAL), delivery-count-based DLQ routing, and a subscriber circuit breaker. Use it for cross-app messaging on CloudHub (VM queues don't cross apps).
  • File-based sources: enable watermarking and avoid deleting/moving the file until downstream processing completes — the file itself is the reprocessing safety net.

Transactions

Single Resource (local)XA (Extended Architecture)
ScopeONE resource: all operations from the same connector using the same global configMULTIPLE resources as one atomic unit (e.g. JMS + Database)
ProtocolNative resource transactionTwo-phase commit (transaction manager required)
PerformanceFastSlower, heavier
NestingNot supportedSupported

Transactional actions (source or Try scope): ALWAYS_BEGIN (start new; error if nesting a single-resource tx), ALWAYS_JOIN (must join an existing tx, error if none), BEGIN_OR_JOIN, JOIN_IF_POSSIBLE (join if present), NOT_SUPPORTED (run outside the tx), INDIFFERENT/NONE.

  • Only transactional connectors participate: JMS, VM, Database (and JDBC-based). HTTP, File, SFTP are NOT transactional.
  • A Try scope starts a transaction inside a flow whose source is non-transactional (e.g. HTTP-triggered flow wrapping two DB writes).
  • Rollback semantics with error handlers: an error reaching an On Error Propagate that is at the level owning the transaction → rollback; handled by On Error Continue → the transaction stays alive and commits (the handler's processors even run inside it). Choosing Continue by accident can commit partial work — classic exam trap.

Performance and scaling

  • Mule 4 uses a single self-tuning thread pool (UBER) with automatic back-pressure — when the app can't keep up, sources are throttled (HTTP returns 503). You rarely size pools; you shape concurrency with maxConcurrency on flows and batch/parallel components.
  • Cache scope: stores the result of its inner processors in an Object Store keyed by a hash of the message (or a custom key). Serve repeated idempotent requests without recomputing; entries need repeatable/consumable payloads and a sensible TTL/invalidation strategy (Invalidate Cache / Invalidate Key operations).
  • Idempotent Message Validator: computes an ID (idExpression) per message, stores seen IDs in an Object Store, and raises MULE:DUPLICATE_MESSAGE for repeats — exactly-once protection at the entry point.
  • Scaling: vertical = larger workers (more vCores/memory per instance); horizontal = more workers/replicas with automatic load balancing (CloudHub) — also the availability play. Stateless apps scale horizontally best; shared state must live in external stores (Object Store v2, DB, MQ), never in memory.
  • Clustering (customer-hosted): nodes form one logical unit over a distributed in-memory grid — persistent object stores and VM queues are shared cluster-wide; scheduled/polling sources run on the primary node only by default to avoid duplicate triggering.

Exam tip: pick the right reliability tool: retry an operation now → Until Successful; survive app crash without losing accepted messages → persistent queue + transaction (reliable acquisition); stop poison messages → redelivery policy + DLQ; atomic write to two systems → XA. And know the streaming defaults (512 KB buffers, 500 objects).

Choosing a streaming strategy

Decide by two questions: how big can the payload get? and how many times is it read?

Read-once passthrough (SFTP → S3) wants non-repeatable (zero buffering). Multiple consumers on bounded data want repeatable in-memory. Multiple consumers on GB-scale data want repeatable file-store. An in-memory buffer that overflows its maxBufferSize raises STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED.

Retry, redelivery, and reconnection compared

ToolRetries whatLevelExhaustion error
Reconnection strategyThe broken connectionConnector config*:CONNECTIVITY
Until SuccessfulThe wrapped processorsFlow scopeMULE:RETRY_EXHAUSTED
Redelivery policyReprocessing the same inbound messageListener/sourceMULE:REDELIVERY_EXHAUSTED

They compose: a reconnection strategy re-establishes a dropped DB connection; Until Successful re-runs a flaky HTTP call; a redelivery policy plus a DLQ stops a poison message from looping forever. Until Successful runs 1 initial attempt + maxRetries — so maxRetries=3 means 4 executions total.

Object Store for state and idempotency

  • Object Store v2 is persistent and shared across all workers of a CloudHub app — the right home for cross-worker, cross-restart state. In-memory stores, flow variables, and static Java fields are per-JVM and lost on restart.
  • The Idempotent Message Validator stores seen IDs in an Object Store and raises MULE:DUPLICATE_MESSAGE on repeats — exactly-once at the entry point.
  • The Cache scope memoizes its inner processors' result by key; on multiple workers use a shared OSv2-backed caching strategy so every worker sees the same cache, and set a TTL to bound staleness. The key must capture everything that makes a response unique (path and query params).

Back-pressure and concurrency

Mule 4's self-tuning engine applies back-pressure when a flow can't keep up: maxConcurrency caps simultaneous executions of a flow, and once the cap is reached the source is throttled — an HTTP Listener answers 503 Service Unavailable rather than queueing indefinitely. Shape load with maxConcurrency and parallel/batch components; you almost never size thread pools by hand.

sections.title: Maintainable and Modular Mule Applications (Maven)

docs.label: Publish assets to Exchange with Maven

objectives: Explain the Maven lifecycle and the POM structure of a Mule project
Use the mule-maven-plugin to package and deploy applications
Manage dependencies, including Mule plugins and shared Java libraries
Create and consume reusable connectors/modules from Exchange
Externalize common configuration with parent POMs and properties
Version assets meaningfully (semantic versioning) and publish to Exchange
Structure applications for reuse: domains, libraries, and API fragments
sections.notes:
POM anatomy of a Mule project

<project>
  <groupId>com.acme</groupId>            <!-- org id when publishing to Exchange -->
  <artifactId>orders-sapi</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
  <packaging>mule-application</packaging>

  <build><plugins>
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.mule.tools.maven</groupId>
      <artifactId>mule-maven-plugin</artifactId>
      <extensions>true</extensions>
      <configuration>
        <sharedLibraries>                 <!-- expose a JAR to connectors -->
          <sharedLibrary>
            <groupId>com.mysql</groupId><artifactId>mysql-connector-j</artifactId>
          </sharedLibrary>
        </sharedLibraries>
        <cloudhub2Deployment>
          <uri>https://anypoint.mulesoft.com</uri>
          <target>Cloudhub-US-East-2</target>
          <environment>${env}</environment>
          <connectedAppClientId>${ca.id}</connectedAppClientId>
          <connectedAppClientSecret>${ca.secret}</connectedAppClientSecret>
          <connectedAppGrantType>client_credentials</connectedAppGrantType>
        </cloudhub2Deployment>
      </configuration>
    </plugin>
  </plugins></build>

  <dependencies>
    <dependency>                          <!-- a connector = mule-plugin -->
      <groupId>org.mule.connectors</groupId>
      <artifactId>mule-http-connector</artifactId>
      <version>1.9.x</version>
      <classifier>mule-plugin</classifier>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>
</project>
  • Classifiers matter: mule-plugin marks Mule extensions/connectors; API specs from Exchange use raml / oas / raml-fragment classifiers; plain Java JARs have none.
  • Shared libraries: ordinary JAR dependencies are isolated from connectors by Mule's classloading. Drivers used BY a connector (JDBC driver, JMS client) must be listed under sharedLibraries or the connector can't see them — a frequent real-world and exam gotcha.

Maven lifecycle (what runs when)

PhaseMule project effect
cleanDelete target/
compileCompile/validate app resources
testRun MUnit suites (munit-maven-plugin), compute coverage
packageBuild the deployable JAR in target/
installCopy artifact to the local repository (~/.m2)
deployPublish to a remote repository (Exchange) — and with -DmuleDeploy, the mule-maven-plugin deploys the app to the configured target instead

Deployment targets supported by the plugin: cloudHubDeployment (CH 1.0), cloudhub2Deployment, rtfDeployment (Runtime Fabric), armDeployment (customer-hosted via Runtime Manager agent), standaloneDeployment. Credentials should be a Connected App (client_credentials grant, minimal scopes) — never personal accounts in CI.

Exchange as a Maven repository

  • Consume: add the Exchange repo URL to the POM/settings and credentials to settings.xml (<server> with connected app or username/token). Exchange assets then resolve like any dependency.
  • Publish: set groupId = your Anypoint organization ID, add Exchange as distributionManagement, run mvn deploy. Works for applications (templates/examples), custom connectors, API specs, and fragments.

Reuse mechanisms — pick the right one

NeedMechanism
Same build config/plugin versions everywhereParent POM (published to Exchange/repo; children use <parent>)
Shared Java utilitiesPlain JAR dependency (+ sharedLibraries if connectors need it)
Reusable flows/operations with versioningCustom Mule plugin / connector (Mule SDK or packaged module) on Exchange
Shared connector configs on one runtimeDomain project (customer-hosted only)
Shared API types/traits/examplesAPI fragments on Exchange, imported by specs
Shared DataWeave functionsDW library module (.dwl) packaged and published as a dependency

Versioning and configuration

  • Semantic versioning MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH: breaking change → MAJOR (new API instance/URL, e.g. /v2); backwards-compatible feature → MINOR; fix → PATCH. Adding an optional field is minor; removing/renaming/retyping is major.
  • Build once, deploy many: one artifact promoted through dev→test→prod. Environment differences via ${env}.yaml property files + deploy-time properties; secrets encrypted (secure properties) or injected by the pipeline; mule-artifact.json's secureProperties list masks values in Runtime Manager.
  • mule-artifact.json also pins minMuleVersion — deployment to an older runtime fails fast.

Exam tip: memorize the lifecycle order and what mvn deploy -DmuleDeploy does vs plain mvn deploy (app deployment vs repository publish). Know why a JDBC driver goes in sharedLibraries, and that Exchange's groupId = organization ID.

Classloader isolation and sharedLibraries

Mule 4 gives every connector/plugin its own isolated classloader, so a connector cannot see JARs on the application classloader. That is why a JDBC driver declared as an ordinary <dependency> still throws ClassNotFoundException when the Database connector initializes — the driver is loaded by the connector's classloader, which can't reach the app's. The fix is to export it via sharedLibraries:

<plugin>
  <groupId>org.mule.tools.maven</groupId>
  <artifactId>mule-maven-plugin</artifactId>
  <configuration>
    <sharedLibraries>
      <sharedLibrary>
        <groupId>com.mysql</groupId>
        <artifactId>mysql-connector-j</artifactId>
      </sharedLibrary>
    </sharedLibraries>
  </configuration>
</plugin>

The <dependency> is still required; sharedLibraries just makes it visible to connectors. Same story for JMS client libraries and other driver-style JARs.

mvn deploy vs. mvn deploy -DmuleDeploy

CommandWhat happens
mvn deployPublishes the artifact to the Maven repository in distributionManagement (e.g. Exchange). The app is NOT deployed to a runtime.
mvn deploy -DmuleDeploySwitches the mule-maven-plugin into deployment mode and deploys the app to the configured target (cloudhub2Deployment, rtfDeployment, armDeployment, standaloneDeployment).

Two very different operations sharing one Maven phase — a favourite exam distinction. CI credentials should be a Connected App (client_credentials grant, minimal scopes), supplied from the pipeline's secret store or settings.xml, never committed to the POM.

Semantic versioning decisions

Change to the API contractBump
Add an optional field or a new resource; add an optional query paramMINOR
Documentation fix, internal refactor, perf improvement (contract unchanged)PATCH
Remove/rename a field, change a type, make an optional field required, remove a resourceMAJOR (new instance, e.g. /v2)

Major versions coexist as separate API instances so consumers migrate on their own schedule; the old version is deprecated with a communicated sunset before removal.

Exam tip: ClassNotFound from a connector → sharedLibraries; publish an asset → groupId = org ID + Exchange in distributionManagement; deploy an app from CI → mule-maven-plugin + -DmuleDeploy + Connected App creds from a secret store.

sections.title: Securing Data
objectives: Configure one-way and two-way (mutual) TLS on HTTP listeners and requesters
Create and use keystores and truststores
Encrypt property files with the Mule Secure Configuration Properties module
Use the Cryptography module to encrypt/decrypt and sign payloads
Apply security-related API policies (OAuth 2.0, JWT validation, client ID enforcement)
Protect sensitive data in transit and at rest across environments
sections.notes:
TLS building blocks

  • A TLS Context (tls:context) is a reusable global element referenced by TLS-capable connectors (HTTP, FTPS, Email, Sockets…). It can hold a keystore (tls:key-store) and/or a truststore (tls:trust-store).
  • Keystore = your identity: private key + certificate. Two passwords: the store's and the key's (keyPassword). Truststore = who you believe: public certificates of trusted parties/CAs. By default Mule trusts the JRE's CA truststore, so public-CA certificates need no custom truststore.
  • Formats: JKS (default), PKCS12, JCEKS — created and managed with keytool (match the JDK version the runtime uses).
ScenarioListener (server) needsRequester (client) needs
Expose HTTPS (one-way)KeystoreTruststore only if the cert isn't publicly trusted
Call HTTPS with private/self-signed cert—Truststore with that cert/CA
Mutual TLSKeystore + truststore, client auth requiredKeystore + truststore
  • insecure="true" on a truststore disables validation — prototyping only, never production.
  • Protocols/ciphers restricted at two levels: runtime (tls-default.conf in $MULE_HOME/conf, or FIPS mode with tls-fips140-2.conf) and app (enabledProtocols, enabledCipherSuites) — a protocol must be enabled in both to be used.

Secure configuration properties

  1. Encrypt values with the Secure Properties Tool JAR: java -cp secure-properties-tool.jar com.mulesoft.tools.SecurePropertiesTool string encrypt AES CBC myKey "myPassword". Methods: string (one value), file (each value in a file), file-level (the whole file). Algorithms: AES (typical), Blowfish, DES(ede)…; modes: CBC (default), CFB, ECB, OFB.
  2. Put the result in the properties file wrapped as ![encryptedValue] — no spaces/characters after the bracket (trailing spaces break decryption): 
    db:
  password: "![kD8s2n...==]"
  3. Add a Secure Properties Config global element (Mule Secure Configuration Properties module): file, key ${secure.key}, algorithm/mode matching the encryption.
  4. Reference with the mandatory secure:: prefix — for every property in a secure file, even unencrypted ones: ${secure::db.password}.
  5. Pass the key at deploy time, never in code: -Dsecure.key=myKey / a hidden Runtime Manager property / CI-injected secret.

mule-artifact.json's secureProperties array additionally masks named properties in Runtime Manager UIs/APIs (masking ≠ encryption — you typically want both).

Payload-level cryptography

The Cryptography module protects data at rest / end-to-end (beyond transport TLS): PGP (encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify with key rings), JCE (symmetric/asymmetric Java crypto), XML (XML encryption/signature). Use when files or messages must remain encrypted wherever they land (e.g. PGP-encrypt a file before SFTP upload).

API security policies (API Manager)

PolicyWhat it validatesNotes
Client ID enforcementclient_id/client_secret of a registered, contracted consumerIdentity of the application; enables SLA tiers
OAuth 2.0 access token enforcementBearer token against the authorization server (introspection)Supports scopes; needs the provider configured
JWT validationSigned JWT locally: signature (JWKS or shared secret), expiry, audience/claimsNo per-request round trip to the provider — lower latency
Basic authenticationUsername/password (simple/LDAP)Legacy/internal use
IP allowlist / blocklistSource IPNetwork-level complement, not identity

Defense in depth: TLS for transport → gateway policies for authN/authZ and abuse control → secure properties for configuration secrets → cryptography for payloads → per-environment keys/certs (never share prod secrets with dev). HTTPS should also protect the connection between a proxy/gateway and the backend ("last mile").

Exam tip: map the failure to the missing piece — handshake fails calling a self-signed service → client truststore; server must identify callers by certificate → mutual TLS (server adds truststore + requires client auth); "decryption fails" with correct key → check ![...] wrapping/trailing spaces and algorithm/mode match; property readable in RM UI → add to secureProperties.

Choosing the right security control

RequirementControl
Encrypt the channel (data in transit)TLS context (HTTPS/FTPS/…)
Server proves its identityKeystore on the server (listener)
Also authenticate the client by certificateMutual TLS: keystore + truststore + client-auth required
Keep config secrets unreadable in Git/artifactSecure Configuration Properties (encrypted ![...], secure::)
Keep a payload/field encrypted at restCryptography module (PGP/JCE/XML)
Identify/authorize API consumersAPI Manager policies (Client ID, OAuth, JWT)

Common trap: TLS protects transit only — a file lands decrypted at the destination. If it must stay encrypted at rest, PGP-encrypt the payload with the recipient's public key before sending.

TLS handshake troubleshooting

SymptomCause & fix
PKIX path building failed / cert not trusted (as client)Add the server's cert/CA to the requester's truststore
Received fatal alert: certificate_required (as client)Server enforces mutual TLS → add a keystore (your client cert + key) to the request's TLS context
Handshake fails exposing HTTPS (as server)Listener missing its keystore (server identity)
Works with insecure="true" onlyTrust is misconfigured — fix the truststore; never ship insecure to prod

Masking vs. encryption of properties

Two independent mechanisms, often used together:

  • Secure Configuration Properties module (in-app) — values are encrypted in the file as ![...] and referenced with secure::; decrypted at runtime with a key supplied at startup (-M-Dmule.key=…). Protects secrets in source control and the artifact.
  • secureProperties array in mule-artifact.json — names deployment properties the platform should mask in the Runtime Manager UI/API and encrypt at rest. Masking ≠ encryption of your config file; you typically want both.

Exam tip: "certificate_required" → client keystore (mutual TLS); "PKIX/not trusted" → client truststore; "readable in RM UI" → secureProperties; "unreadable in Git" → Secure Properties module; "encrypted at rest after SFTP" → PGP via Cryptography module.

sections.title: Building Monitorable Mule Applications
objectives: Configure application logging with Log4j 2 (levels, categories, appenders, async logging)
Write effective, structured log messages with correlation IDs
Use Anypoint Monitoring: built-in and custom dashboards, log search, alerts
Create alerts in Runtime Manager and API Manager (e.g. response time, policy violations)
Track business events and use the Custom Business Event component
Expose application health for external monitoring
sections.notes:
Log4j 2 configuration

Each app owns src/main/resources/log4j2.xml: appenders (where logs go) and loggers (which categories log at which level).

<Configuration>
  <Appenders>
    <RollingFile name="file" fileName="${sys:mule.home}/logs/orders-api.log" ...>
      <PatternLayout pattern="%d [%t] %-5p %c - %m%n"/>
    </RollingFile>
    <!-- ship to an external system: Http, Socket, or a Splunk/ELK appender -->
  </Appenders>
  <Loggers>
    <AsyncLogger name="com.acme.orders" level="DEBUG"/>   <!-- your categories -->
    <AsyncLogger name="org.mule.runtime.core.internal.processor.LoggerMessageProcessor"
                 level="INFO"/>                            <!-- Logger component output -->
    <AsyncRoot level="INFO"><AppenderRef ref="file"/></AsyncRoot>
  </Loggers>
</Configuration>
  • Levels: ERROR > WARN > INFO > DEBUG > TRACE — a category set to INFO suppresses its DEBUG/TRACE. Tune noisy third-party categories down, your own up, without touching flows.
  • Sync vs async: async loggers/appenders don't block event processing → higher throughput, but buffered entries can be lost on a crash and strict ordering weakens. Synchronous logging is the safe-but-slower choice for audit-critical logs.
  • Logger component: message (DataWeave), level, and category (e.g. com.acme.orders.payment) — always set meaningful categories so operators can filter/level them per environment.
  • CloudHub: the platform captures stdout logs with retention limits; to use custom appenders/external log systems, disable CloudHub logs for the app (checkbox in Runtime Manager, subject to org permissions) and ship via your appender. On CloudHub 2.0/RTF, sidecars/log forwarding fill this role.

Correlation and structure

  • Every event has a correlationId, printed by default log patterns. It propagates across apps via the X-Correlation-ID HTTP header (HTTP connector sends/reads it automatically) — search it in centralized logs to trace one business transaction end-to-end through the application network.
  • Prefer structured (JSON) log messages with stable fields (event, orderId, status) — machines parse them, dashboards aggregate them. Log at boundaries: received / calling X / completed / failed. Never log credentials or full sensitive payloads; mask PII.

Anypoint Monitoring

CapabilityWhat you get
Built-in dashboardsPer-app metrics out of the box: request volume, response times, error rates, CPU/memory, connector performance
Custom dashboardsCompose charts over any collected metrics (per app, API, or business metric)
Log managementCentralized log search across apps; longer retention and advanced features with the Titanium subscription
AlertsThreshold alerts on metrics (e.g. avg response time > 2s for 5 min, error ratio > 5%)
Functional monitoring (BAT)Scheduled black-box tests that call endpoints (from public/private locations) and assert on responses — uptime and behavior from the consumer's perspective

Anypoint Visualizer renders the real application network graph (who calls whom) from traffic data — useful for impact analysis and architecture reviews.

Alerts in Runtime Manager vs API Manager

Runtime Manager (operational)API Manager (API-level)
Application down / not respondingPolicy violation (e.g. rate-limit exceeded)
Worker not responding / restartedRequest count threshold
Deployment success/failureResponse time threshold
Custom application notificationsResponse codes (4xx/5xx ratios)

Alert channels: email to platform users/arbitrary addresses; alerts have severity and can be scoped per app/API instance and environment.

Business-level tracking

  • Custom Business Event component: publishes a named milestone (e.g. "OrderPlaced") with key/value metadata (order id, amount) — visible in monitoring/analytics, independent of technical logs. Default flow/event tracking can also be enabled per flow ("transaction id" for business correlation).
  • Health for external monitors: expose a lightweight /health (or use API Functional Monitoring) returning dependency status — load balancers and uptime checks probe it.

Exam tip: know WHERE each thing is configured — app logging: log4j2.xml; app down alert: Runtime Manager; policy-violation alert: API Manager; cross-app log search: Anypoint Monitoring (Titanium for long retention); tracing one transaction across apps: correlation ID via X-Correlation-ID.

Where each alert lives

You want to be alerted when…Configured in
The application stops / a worker is unresponsive / deployment failed / CPU or memory thresholdRuntime Manager (and Anypoint Monitoring metric alerts)
An API's response time, error rate, request count, or a policy violation crosses a thresholdAPI Manager alerts
You need to search logs across many apps in one placeAnypoint Monitoring log management (Titanium)
A scheduled black-box test of a live endpoint failsAPI Functional Monitoring (BAT)

Rule of thumb: infrastructure/app lifecycle → Runtime Manager; API behaviour → API Manager; centralized search & metrics → Anypoint Monitoring.

What to log (and what not to)

  • Do: log at flow boundaries with the correlation ID; use meaningful categories (com.acme.orders.payment) so levels can be tuned per environment; prefer structured (JSON) messages for aggregation.
  • Don't: log entire payloads with PII/credentials at INFO — it leaks data and bloats storage. Reserve payload dumps for guarded DEBUG, and mask sensitive fields.
  • Async vs sync: async loggers boost throughput (I/O off the processing thread) but can lose the last buffered entries on a hard crash and weaken strict ordering — keep audit/ERROR categories synchronous when durability matters.

Anypoint Monitoring vs. Functional Monitoring

  • Anypoint Monitoring — dashboards, metrics, and (Titanium) centralized log management for deployed apps/APIs. Answers "how is it behaving right now?"
  • API Functional Monitoring (BAT) — scheduled black-box tests that call endpoints from chosen locations and assert on responses, alerting on failures. Answers "is it working from the consumer's perspective?"
  • Custom Business Events — publish named business milestones ("OrderPlaced") with key/value metadata for business-level KPIs in Monitoring, distinct from technical logs/metrics.

Exam tip: app down → Runtime Manager; 5xx rate / policy violation → API Manager; cross-app log search → Anypoint Monitoring; endpoint health from outside → Functional Monitoring; business KPI → Custom Business Event; trace one transaction across apps → correlation ID.

sections.title: Production-Ready APIs and Automated Testing
objectives: Test applications with MUnit: test suites, asserts, mocks, spies
Set MUnit coverage requirements and run tests in a Maven build
Prepare APIs for production: autodiscovery, policies, contracts
Implement API versioning and deprecation strategies
Integrate builds and deployments into CI/CD pipelines
Govern APIs with Exchange documentation and API instances per environment
sections.notes:
MUnit test structure

Suites are XML files in src/test/munit; each test has three blocks — Behavior (arrange), Execution (act), Validation (assert):

<munit:test name="getFlights-returns-200">
  <munit:behavior>
    <munit-tools:mock-when processor="db:select">
      <munit-tools:with-attributes>
        <munit-tools:with-attribute attributeName="doc:name" whereValue="Select flights"/>
      </munit-tools:with-attributes>
      <munit-tools:then-return>
        <munit-tools:payload value='#[[{"code":"ER38sd"},{"code":"ER45if"}]]'/>
      </munit-tools:then-return>
    </munit-tools:mock-when>
  </munit:behavior>
  <munit:execution>
    <flow-ref name="getFlightsFlow"/>
  </munit:execution>
  <munit:validation>
    <munit-tools:assert-that expression="#[sizeOf(payload)]"
        is="#[MunitTools::equalTo(2)]"/>
  </munit:validation>
</munit:test>
ComponentPurpose
Mock WhenReplace a processor (matched by name + attributes like doc:name) with a canned payload/attributes/variables — or Then throw an error (typeId e.g. HTTP:CONNECTIVITY) to drive error handlers deterministically
SpyAssert on the event before and/or after a real processor runs, without replacing it
Verify CallAssert a processor was invoked N times / at least / at most
Assert That / Assert Equals / FailAssertions with MunitTools matchers: equalTo, notNullValue, hasSize, containsString, hasKey…
Before/After Suite & Before/After TestOne-time vs per-test setup/teardown
Set EventBuild the input event (payload, attributes, vars) for the flow under test
  • Testing an HTTP-triggered flow: either flow-ref the flow directly with a Set Event that mimics listener attributes, or enable flow sources and call the real endpoint with an HTTP Request inside the test.
  • Tests can be tagged, ignored, or parameterized (run the same suite over multiple input files).

Coverage and the Maven build

<plugin>
  <groupId>com.mulesoft.munit.tools</groupId>
  <artifactId>munit-maven-plugin</artifactId>
  <configuration>
    <coverage>
      <runCoverage>true</runCoverage>
      <failBuild>true</failBuild>
      <requiredApplicationCoverage>80</requiredApplicationCoverage>
      <requiredResourceCoverage>70</requiredResourceCoverage>
      <requiredFlowCoverage>60</requiredFlowCoverage>
      <formats><format>html</format><format>json</format></formats>
    </coverage>
  </configuration>
</plugin>
  • MUnit runs in the test phase (mvn test); coverage thresholds at application/resource/flow level can fail the build — the standard CI quality gate. Tests are excluded from the deployable JAR.
  • Typical pipeline: commit → mvn clean package (compiles + MUnit + coverage) → publish artifact/spec to Exchange → mvn deploy -DmuleDeploy per environment with injected properties/secrets → smoke/functional tests → promote. Same artifact every stage; Connected App credentials; secure keys from the secret store.

Production readiness checklist

  • One API instance per environment in API Manager; the app pairs via autodiscovery with an environment-specific api.id property. Policies/SLAs configured per instance.
  • Baseline policies: an identity policy (client ID enforcement / OAuth / JWT) + rate limiting or spike control; TLS on all endpoints; secure properties for secrets.
  • Exchange documentation: portal pages, examples, terms; enable the mocking service for evaluation; consumers self-serve access via contracts and SLA tiers.
  • Operational: alerts (RM + API Manager), dashboards, correlation-friendly logging, health endpoint, scaling plan (workers/replicas), and MUnit coverage gate in CI.

Versioning and deprecation

  • Only MAJOR versions surface to consumers (URL segment /v2 or new instance); MINOR/PATCH stay backwards-compatible. Additive changes (new optional field/resource) must not break clients; contract-breaking changes require a new major version.
  • Deprecation flow: release v2 alongside v1 → mark v1 deprecated in Exchange/API Manager (blocks/flags new access requests, signals consumers) → notify contract holders with a sunset date → monitor v1 traffic → retire after the window. Never silently redirect or delete while contracts are active.

Exam tip: Mock When vs Spy vs Verify is the most-tested MUnit distinction: replace = Mock, observe without replacing = Spy, count calls = Verify. Also memorize the coverage config knobs (failBuild, requiredApplicationCoverage) and that MUnit binds to the Maven test phase.

MUnit tools: mock, spy, verify, assert

ToolPurposeReplaces the processor?
Mock WhenReturn a canned payload/attributes — or throw a typed error — instead of running a processor (matched by name/attributes)Yes
SpyAssert on the event immediately before and/or after a processor runsNo (it still executes)
Verify CallAssert how many times a processor was invoked (times/atLeast/atMost)No
Assert ThatCheck an expression against a MunitTools matcher (equalTo, notNullValue, hasSize…)No

A test is structured Behavior → Execution → Validation (arrange/mocks → invoke the flow → assert). Mock When's then-throw is how you drive error handlers deterministically (e.g. raise HTTP:CONNECTIVITY) instead of relying on a real failure. Expect a specific error from the flow with expectedErrorType on the munit:test.

Coverage as a build gate

<plugin>
  <groupId>com.mulesoft.munit.tools</groupId>
  <artifactId>munit-maven-plugin</artifactId>
  <configuration><coverage>
    <runCoverage>true</runCoverage>
    <failBuild>true</failBuild>
    <requiredApplicationCoverage>80</requiredApplicationCoverage>
  </coverage></configuration>
</plugin>

Tests live in src/test/munit, run in the Maven test phase, and are not packaged into the deployable JAR. Before/After Suite run once per suite; Before/After Test run around each test.

Deprecating an API version gracefully

A breaking RAML change means a new MAJOR version and a new API instance (e.g. /v2). The old version: mark deprecated in Exchange/API Manager → stop new access requests → notify existing contract holders with a sunset date → retire after the window. Never delete v1 the moment v2 ships, and never silently redirect v1 traffic to v2.

Exam tip: replace a call → Mock When; force an error → Mock When then-throw; count invocations → Verify Call; check a value → Assert That; observe without replacing → Spy; one-time data setup → Before Suite; build-once/deploy-many and gate on coverage before promotion.

cards.front: Repeatable file-stored stream: default in-memory buffer and edition?
cards.back: 512 KB buffer, then overflow to disk. Default on Enterprise Edition.

cards.front: Repeatable in-memory stream: defaults and failure mode?
cards.back: Initial 512 KB, +512 KB increments up to maxInMemorySize; exceeding it raises STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED. Default on Community Edition/Kernel.

cards.front: Streamed OBJECTS (auto-paging connectors): default buffer size?
cards.back: 500 objects (in-memory, +100 increments); EE file-store variant serializes overflow with Kryo.

cards.front: DataWeave streaming: reader and writer properties?
cards.back: Reader: streaming=true (sequential single-pass). Writer: deferred=true (stream output downstream).

cards.front: Can VM queues connect two different Mule applications?
cards.back: NO — VM queues are intra-application only. Use Anypoint MQ or JMS between apps.

cards.front: Anypoint MQ: FIFO queues, exchanges, ack modes?
cards.back: FIFO queues = ordering; message exchanges = pub/sub fan-out; ack modes IMMEDIATE / AUTO / MANUAL; DLQ routing by delivery count.

cards.front: Local (single resource) transaction constraints?
cards.back: All operations from the SAME connector using the SAME global config; no nesting. XA (two-phase commit) spans multiple resources, supports nesting, slower.

cards.front: Which connectors are transactional? Which are NOT?
cards.back: Transactional: JMS, VM, Database (JDBC). NOT: HTTP, File, SFTP.

cards.front: Error reaches On Error CONTINUE inside a transactional flow — what happens to the transaction?
cards.back: It stays alive and COMMITS (handler even runs inside it). Propagate at the tx-owning level → ROLLBACK. Continue can silently commit partial work!

cards.front: Reconnection strategy vs Until Successful vs redelivery policy?
cards.back: Reconnection = CONNECTION-level retry (connector config). Until Successful = retries wrapped OPERATIONS in memory (RETRY_EXHAUSTED). Redelivery policy = per-MESSAGE failure count on a source (REDELIVERY_EXHAUSTED → DLQ).

cards.front: Transactional actions: ALWAYS_BEGIN vs BEGIN_OR_JOIN vs ALWAYS_JOIN?
cards.back: ALWAYS_BEGIN: start new (error if nesting single-resource tx). BEGIN_OR_JOIN: join if present else start. ALWAYS_JOIN: must join existing, error if none.

cards.front: A JDBC driver added as a normal dependency throws ClassNotFoundException in the connector. Why + fix?
cards.back: Mule 4 classloader isolation hides app JARs from connectors. Declare it under sharedLibraries in the mule-maven-plugin.

cards.front: mvn deploy vs mvn deploy -DmuleDeploy?
cards.back: Plain: publish artifact to the Maven repository (e.g., Exchange). With -DmuleDeploy: deploy the APP to the configured target (CloudHub/RTF/etc.).

cards.front: Maven lifecycle: which phase runs MUnit?
cards.back: test (via munit-maven-plugin) — so mvn package already runs tests and coverage gates.

cards.front: groupId when publishing to Exchange? Classifier for connectors?
cards.back: groupId = your Anypoint ORGANIZATION ID. Connectors/modules use classifier 'mule-plugin'.

cards.front: Semver: adding an optional field vs renaming a field?
cards.back: Optional addition = MINOR (backwards compatible). Rename/remove/retype = MAJOR (breaking — new /v2 instance).

cards.front: What does mule-artifact.json declare?
cards.back: minMuleVersion (deploy fails fast on older runtimes) and secureProperties (masked in Runtime Manager UI/API).

cards.front: Keystore vs truststore?
cards.back: Keystore = YOUR identity (private key + cert). Truststore = who you TRUST (public certs/CAs). Default keystore type: JKS.

cards.front: One-way vs two-way TLS: who needs what?
cards.back: One-way: server keystore; client truststore (only if cert not publicly trusted). Two-way: BOTH sides have keystore + truststore; server requires client certs.

cards.front: What does insecure="true" on a truststore do?
cards.back: Disables certificate validation entirely — prototyping/testing ONLY, never production.

cards.front: Secure properties: value wrapper syntax, prefix, and default cipher mode?
cards.back: Encrypted values wrapped as ![...] (no trailing spaces!). Reference with mandatory secure:: prefix (all props in a secure file). Default mode CBC; typical algorithm AES.

cards.front: Where should the secure-properties decryption key come from?
cards.back: Deploy time: -Dsecure.key VM arg, hidden Runtime Manager property, or CI-injected secret — NEVER packaged with the app.

cards.front: A file must stay encrypted at rest on a partner's SFTP. TLS enough?
cards.back: No — TLS protects transit only. PGP-encrypt the payload (Cryptography module) with the partner's PUBLIC key; only their private key decrypts.

cards.front: JWT validation policy vs OAuth token enforcement?
cards.back: JWT: validated LOCALLY (signature via JWKS, expiry, claims) — no per-request round trip. OAuth enforcement: introspects the token against the authorization server each request.

cards.front: Async loggers: benefit and trade-off?
cards.back: Higher throughput/lower latency, BUT buffered entries can be lost on a crash and strict ordering weakens. Keep audit-critical logs synchronous.

cards.front: How does the correlation ID cross apps? Where do you see it?
cards.back: Propagated automatically via the X-Correlation-ID HTTP header; included in default log patterns — search it in centralized logs to trace one transaction.

cards.front: Where to configure: app-down alert / policy-violation alert / cross-app log search?
cards.back: App down: Runtime Manager. Policy violation & API response codes: API Manager. Log search across apps: Anypoint Monitoring log management (Titanium for long retention).

cards.front: What is the Custom Business Event component for?
cards.back: Publishing named business milestones (e.g., 'OrderPlaced') with key/value metadata — business-level tracking independent of technical logs.

cards.front: MUnit: Mock When vs Spy vs Verify Call?
cards.back: Mock When = REPLACE a processor (then-return / then-throw). Spy = observe before/after WITHOUT replacing. Verify Call = assert invocation count (times/atLeast/atMost).

cards.front: MUnit test structure blocks?
cards.back: Behavior (arrange: mocks, set event) → Execution (act: flow-ref) → Validation (assert-that with MunitTools matchers).

cards.front: How to force an error path in MUnit deterministically?
cards.back: Mock When on the processor with 'Then throw', raising a typed error (e.g., typeId HTTP:CONNECTIVITY).

cards.front: Coverage build gate configuration?
cards.back: munit-maven-plugin coverage block: runCoverage, failBuild=true, requiredApplicationCoverage / requiredResourceCoverage / requiredFlowCoverage.

cards.front: Build-once principle in CI/CD?
cards.back: Build and test ONE artifact, publish it, and promote the SAME JAR through environments — env differences come from externalized properties/secrets at deploy time.

cards.front: Deprecation flow for a breaking API change?
cards.back: Release v2 as a NEW instance alongside v1 → mark v1 deprecated (Exchange/API Manager) → notify contract holders with a sunset date → monitor traffic → retire.

exercises.title: Reliable acquisition: never lose an accepted order
exercises.task:
An HTTP-triggered flow accepts orders but must never lose one, even if the downstream ERP call fails or the app restarts mid-processing. Build the reliable acquisition pattern: accept + persist first, process asynchronously afterwards.

  • An acquisition flow that receives the order, writes it to a persistent VM queue, and immediately returns 202 Accepted.
  • A separate processing flow whose VM Listener consumes the queue with transactionalAction="ALWAYS_BEGIN" and calls a (failable) ERP subflow.

Then prove it: make the ERP call fail and confirm the message returns to the queue instead of being lost.

given.label: Acquisition flow (sketch)
given.code:
<flow name="accept-order">
