  <http:listener path="/orders"/>
  <!-- publish to VM, then respond 202 -->
  <http:response statusCode="202"/>
</flow>

given.label: Failable ERP subflow
given.code:
<sub-flow name="callErp">
  <raise-error type="APP:ERP_DOWN"/>   <!-- toggle to simulate an outage -->
</sub-flow>

steps: Create a VM config with a PERSISTENT queue named 'orders'
Acquisition flow: vm:publish to 'orders', then set the HTTP response to 202
Processing flow: vm:listener on 'orders' with transactionalAction=ALWAYS_BEGIN, then flow-ref callErp
Run with the ERP subflow raising an error; watch the message roll back and redeliver
Remove the raise-error and confirm the order is processed exactly once

solution.label: app.xml (essential parts)
solution.code:
<vm:config name="VM">
  <vm:queues>
    <vm:queue queueName="orders" queueType="PERSISTENT"/>
  </vm:queues>
</vm:config>

<flow name="accept-order">
  <http:listener config-ref="HTTP" path="/orders"/>
  <vm:publish config-ref="VM" queueName="orders"/>
  <set-payload value='#[{accepted: true}]'/>
  <http:response statusCode="202"/>
</flow>

<flow name="process-order">
  <vm:listener config-ref="VM" queueName="orders"
               transactionalAction="ALWAYS_BEGIN"/>
  <flow-ref name="callErp"/>
  <logger message="#['delivered ' ++ (payload.id default '?')]"/>
</flow>

exercises.solutionNotes: The pattern splits a non-transactional source (HTTP) from transactional processing. Because the VM queue is persistent and the listener begins a transaction, a failure anywhere in process-order rolls the message back onto the queue for redelivery — the caller already got its 202, so nothing is lost. Contrast with calling the ERP directly inside accept-order: an outage there would fail the client's request and drop the order.

exercises.title: Bound a fragile backend with Until Successful + idempotency
exercises.task: A flow calls a flaky payment API that occasionally returns transient errors. Requirement: retry it a few times before giving up, but the payment operation is not idempotent, so a naive retry could double-charge. Build a retrying call that is safe.

given.label: Given
given.code:
- Payment API: POST /charge  (sometimes 503, succeeds on retry)
- Each request carries a unique orderId in the payload
- A double charge for the same orderId is unacceptable

steps: Wrap the HTTP Request in an Until Successful scope (maxRetries=3, millisBetweenRetries=2000)
Make the call idempotent from the API's side by sending an idempotency key header derived from orderId
Decide what to do when retries are exhausted (MULE:RETRY_EXHAUSTED) — route to a DLQ or alert
Confirm: on a transient 503 it retries; on repeated success for the same key the backend charges once

solution.label: Flow fragment
solution.code:
<until-successful maxRetries="3" millisBetweenRetries="2000">
  <http:request method="POST" path="/charge" config-ref="Payment">
    <http:headers>#[{ 'Idempotency-Key': payload.orderId }]</http:headers>
  </http:request>
</until-successful>
<error-handler>
  <on-error-propagate type="MULE:RETRY_EXHAUSTED">
    <vm:publish queueName="payment-dlq" config-ref="VM"/>
  </on-error-propagate>
</error-handler>

exercises.solutionNotes: Until Successful runs 1 initial attempt + maxRetries, so maxRetries=3 means up to 4 calls; exhaustion raises MULE:RETRY_EXHAUSTED. Retrying a non-idempotent operation is only safe if the receiver can dedupe — hence the idempotency key. Retries alone don't make an operation safe; the key is what prevents the double charge.

exercises.title: Choose and configure a streaming strategy for a 2 GB file
exercises.task: A flow reads a 2 GB CSV from SFTP, and two downstream components each need to read the full stream. Configure streaming so the app doesn't run out of memory and both consumers can read it. Then change the requirement to a single read-once passthrough and adjust.

given.label: Given
given.code:
- Source: <sftp:read path="huge.csv"/>
- Consumer 1: a DataWeave transform
- Consumer 2: a Logger of a summary
- Worker memory is limited (well under 2 GB)

steps: Because two components read it, the stream must be REPEATABLE — pick repeatable file-stored streaming
Set a small inMemorySize so most of the 2 GB spills to disk instead of RAM
Then, for the alternative single-read case, switch to a non-repeatable stream and explain the trade-off

solution.label: Repeatable, two readers
solution.code:
<sftp:read path="huge.csv" config-ref="SFTP">
  <repeatable-file-store-stream inMemorySize="1" bufferUnit="MB"/>
</sftp:read>
<!-- both the Transform and the Logger can now read the payload -->

solution.label: Non-repeatable, single reader
solution.code:
<sftp:read path="huge.csv" config-ref="SFTP">
  <non-repeatable-stream/>
</sftp:read>
<!-- fastest, lowest memory; the payload can be read ONCE -->

exercises.solutionNotes: Two questions pick the strategy: how big? and read how many times? Multiple reads → repeatable (buffered so it can replay); huge payload → file-store variant with a small in-memory threshold so it overflows to disk. Read-once passthrough → non-repeatable (zero buffering, lowest memory) — but a second read then fails. Logging #[payload] mid-flow secretly consumes a non-repeatable stream: a classic exam trap.

exercises.title: Atomic write to two systems with an XA transaction
exercises.task: A flow must consume a JMS message and insert derived rows into a database so that both commit or both roll back — a failure after the DB insert must return the message to the queue. Configure it correctly, then prove the rollback.

given.label: Given
given.code:
- Source: JMS listener on queue 'events'
- Then: <db:insert> into an audit table
- Then: a step that can fail (raise APP:BOOM to test)
- Requirement: JMS redelivery AND DB rollback together

steps: Configure BOTH the JMS and Database connectors for XA transactions
Set the JMS listener's transactionalAction to ALWAYS_BEGIN (it starts the XA transaction)
Put the db:insert and the failing step inside that transactional scope
Raise APP:BOOM after the insert; confirm the row is NOT committed and the JMS message is redelivered

solution.label: Flow (essential parts)
solution.code:
<jms:listener config-ref="JMS_XA" destination="events"
              transactionalAction="ALWAYS_BEGIN"
              transactionType="XA"/>
<db:insert config-ref="DB_XA">
  <db:sql>INSERT INTO audit(payload) VALUES(:p)</db:sql>
  <db:input-parameters>#[{ p: write(payload,'application/json') }]</db:input-parameters>
</db:insert>
<raise-error type="APP:BOOM"/>   <!-- both JMS + DB roll back -->

exercises.solutionNotes: An XA (two-phase commit) transaction spans multiple resources as one atomic unit — only transactional connectors (JMS, VM, Database) can enrol; HTTP/File/SFTP cannot. The source with ALWAYS_BEGIN owns the transaction; an error reaching an On Error Propagate at that level rolls everything back, so the DB insert is undone and the JMS message redelivered. Watch the trap: an On Error Continue would commit the partial work instead.

exercises.title: Standardize builds with a Mule parent POM
exercises.task: Three Mule projects duplicate the same plugin versions, repository definitions, and common properties. Create a parent POM they can all inherit from so those are declared once, and show how a child references it.
steps: Create a new Maven module with <packaging>pom</packaging> holding the shared properties, pluginManagement, and repositories
Deploy/install the parent so children can resolve it
In each child pom.xml, add a <parent> element pointing at it and delete the now-inherited blocks
Confirm a child still builds with mvn clean package, inheriting everything
exercises.solutionNotes: A pom-packaged parent is the standard Maven mechanism for organization-wide consistency: declare plugin versions, repositories, and properties once and inherit them everywhere. Children override only what differs. This is exam-relevant for 'maintainable and modular' — the alternative (copy-paste) is exactly the drift the objective warns against.

exercises.title: Publish and consume a reusable module via Exchange
exercises.task: Twelve apps re-implement the same error-response formatting and logging. Package it once as a reusable Mule plugin/library, publish it to Exchange, and consume it from another app as a dependency — no copy-paste.
steps: Create a Mule project containing the shared flows; give it a groupId/artifactId and a proper version
Configure Exchange as the distribution repository and run mvn deploy to publish it as a reusable asset
In a consumer app, add it as a dependency (from Exchange) in pom.xml
Reference the imported sub-flow from the consumer's flows and build

solution.label: Library pom.xml (publish)
solution.code:
<distributionManagement>
  <repository>
    <id>exchange</id>
    <url>https://maven.anypoint.mulesoft.com/api/v3/organizations/${orgId}/maven</url>
  </repository>
</distributionManagement>
<!-- then: mvn deploy  (authenticated via a connected app / settings.xml) -->

exercises.solutionNotes: Publishing shared code as a versioned Exchange asset gives controlled reuse, discoverability, and upgrade management across teams — the opposite of copy-paste drift. The mule-plugin classifier makes the artifact's flows/configs importable; consumers pin a version and upgrade deliberately. This is the 'modular applications' story the exam expects, and it's why one giant app-of-everything is the wrong answer.

exercises.title: Encrypt secrets with the Secure Properties module
exercises.task: A properties file holds a plaintext database password committed to Git. Move it to encrypted secure properties so the artifact is safe to share, with the decryption key supplied at deploy time (never in the repo).

given.label: config.yaml (before)
given.code:
db:
  user: app
  password: S3cr3tP@ss   # plaintext, in Git — must be fixed

steps: Encrypt the value with the Secure Properties Tool (algorithm Blowfish/AES) to get an ![...] ciphertext
Store the ciphertext in config.yaml and reference it via a Secure Configuration Properties element
Read the value in the app with ${secure::db.password}
Supply the key ONLY at deploy time via -Dsecure.key=... (or a hidden platform property), never committed

solution.label: app.xml + run
solution.code:
<secure-properties:config name="Secure_Props"
    file="config.yaml" key="${secure.key}">
  <secure-properties:encrypt algorithm="Blowfish" mode="CBC"/>
</secure-properties:config>

<db:config ...><db:my-connection password="${secure::db.password}"/></db:config>

<!-- deploy:  -Dsecure.key=THEKEY   (or a hidden CloudHub property) -->

exercises.solutionNotes: The whole point of encrypting properties is defeated if the key travels with the artifact (or in the same repo). The ![...] ciphertext is safe to commit; the key is supplied out-of-band — a startup argument, CI/CD secret, or platform property marked hidden. Reference encrypted values with the secure:: prefix. A common failure to recognize: Could not resolve placeholder secure::db.password means the key/config wasn't provided.

exercises.title: Configure two-way TLS for a partner mutual-auth API
exercises.task: An HTTP Request to a partner API fails the TLS handshake with Received fatal alert: certificate_required. The partner enforces mutual TLS. Configure a TLS context so your app presents a client certificate and trusts the partner's CA.

given.label: Given
given.code:
- keystore.jks : your client key + cert (present to the partner)
- truststore.jks : the partner's CA cert (to validate their server)
- Current config has NO tls:context on the requester

steps: Add a tls:context to the HTTP Request config
Configure tls:key-store (your identity) so the app can present a client certificate
Configure tls:trust-store (the partner's CA) so the app validates the partner's server cert
Re-run and confirm the handshake completes

solution.label: HTTP Request config with mTLS
solution.code:
<http:request-config name="Partner">
  <http:request-connection host="partner.example.com" port="443" protocol="HTTPS">
    <tls:context>
      <tls:key-store type="jks" path="keystore.jks"
                     keyPassword="${secure::ks.key}" password="${secure::ks.pass}"/>
      <tls:trust-store type="jks" path="truststore.jks"
                       password="${secure::ts.pass}"/>
    </tls:context>
  </http:request-connection>
</http:request-config>

exercises.solutionNotes: Mutual TLS needs both halves: the key-store holds your identity (the client cert you present — its absence caused certificate_required), and the trust-store holds the CA you use to validate the partner's server. One-way TLS configures only the trust-store. Note the passwords themselves are pulled from secure properties, tying this exercise back to secrets management.

exercises.title: Structured logging: async loggers, levels, and a JSON layout
exercises.task: Tune an app's log4j2.xml: make application logs async for throughput, quiet the runtime to WARN, keep your package at INFO, and emit a layout that includes the correlation ID so one transaction can be traced across apps.

given.label: Requirements
given.code:
- com.acme.orders  -> INFO, asynchronous
- org.mule.runtime -> WARN
- every log line must include the correlationId
- audit logger com.acme.audit must stay SYNCHRONOUS (no loss on crash)

steps: Use AsyncLogger for com.acme.orders and a plain Logger for com.acme.audit
Set org.mule.runtime to WARN
Add %X{correlationId} to the PatternLayout (or use a JSON layout)
Explain the async trade-off you are accepting

solution.label: log4j2.xml (essential parts)
solution.code:
<Appenders>
  <RollingFile name="file" fileName="app.log" ...>
    <PatternLayout pattern="%d %-5p [%X{correlationId}] %c{1} - %m%n"/>
  </RollingFile>
</Appenders>
<Loggers>
  <AsyncLogger name="com.acme.orders" level="INFO"/>
  <Logger name="com.acme.audit" level="INFO" additivity="false">
    <AppenderRef ref="file"/>
  </Logger>
  <AsyncLogger name="org.mule.runtime" level="WARN"/>
  <Root level="INFO"><AppenderRef ref="file"/></Root>
</Loggers>

exercises.solutionNotes: %X{correlationId} pulls Mule's correlation ID from the thread context so a single search returns a transaction's lines across apps (Mule propagates it via the X-Correlation-ID header). Async loggers raise throughput and lower latency, but buffered entries can be lost on a crash and strict ordering weakens — so audit-critical logs stay synchronous. That selective-sync decision is the exam's favourite async-logging nuance.

exercises.title: Emit a business-level metric with Custom Business Events
exercises.task: Operations wants to alert when fewer than 100 orders/hour flow through the platform — a business signal, independent of CPU/HTTP health. Instrument the order flow so this can be tracked and alerted in Anypoint Monitoring.

given.label: Given
given.code:
- The order flow processes each order and knows payload.orderId and payload.amount
- Alert target: orders-per-hour and total order value

steps: Add a Custom Business Event (tracking:transaction) to the order flow
Attach tracking metadata: the orderId (KPI identifier) and amount (a numeric tracking value)
Explain where this surfaces (Anypoint Monitoring business events / custom dashboards) vs. infra metrics
Note what would NOT work: a CPU alert says nothing about order volume

solution.label: Flow fragment
solution.code:
<flow name="process-order">
  ...
  <tracking:transaction name="OrderProcessed">
    <tracking:meta-data key="orderId" value="#[payload.orderId]"/>
    <tracking:meta-data key="amount"  value="#[payload.amount]"/>
  </tracking:transaction>
</flow>

exercises.solutionNotes: Custom Business Events attach business KPIs (order id, value) to a transaction so they surface in Anypoint Monitoring dashboards and alerts — the right tool when the requirement is about business throughput, not technical health. Infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, HTTP 5xx) cannot answer 'are enough orders flowing?'. Recognizing that gap is exactly what the monitoring objective tests.

exercises.title: Write an MUnit test with Mock When and Assert That
exercises.task: Unit-test getFlightsFlow without a real database. Mock the db:select to return two canned rows, run the flow, and assert the response has size 2 — all with no broker or DB.

given.label: Flow under test
given.code:
<flow name="getFlightsFlow">
  <http:listener path="/flights"/>
  <db:select doc:name="Select flights" config-ref="DB">
    <db:sql>SELECT code FROM flights</db:sql>
  </db:select>
</flow>

steps: Create a suite under src/test/munit
Behavior: mock-when the db:select (matched by doc:name) to then-return a 2-element payload
Execution: flow-ref getFlightsFlow
Validation: assert-that sizeOf(payload) equalTo 2

solution.label: getFlights-test.xml
solution.code:
<munit:test name="getFlights-returns-200">
  <munit:behavior>
    <munit-tools:mock-when processor="db:select">
      <munit-tools:with-attributes>
        <munit-tools:with-attribute attributeName="doc:name" whereValue="Select flights"/>
      </munit-tools:with-attributes>
      <munit-tools:then-return>
        <munit-tools:payload value='#[[{code:"ER38sd"},{code:"ER45if"}]]'/>
      </munit-tools:then-return>
    </munit-tools:mock-when>
  </munit:behavior>
  <munit:execution>
    <flow-ref name="getFlightsFlow"/>
  </munit:execution>
  <munit:validation>
    <munit-tools:assert-that expression="#[sizeOf(payload)]"
        is="#[MunitTools::equalTo(2)]"/>
  </munit:validation>
</munit:test>

exercises.solutionNotes: The MUnit shape is Behavior → Execution → Validation (arrange/mock → act → assert). Mock When replaces a processor matched by name/attributes with a canned result, so the DB never runs; Assert That checks an expression against a MunitTools matcher. To drive an error path instead, Mock When's then-throw raises a typed error (e.g. DB:CONNECTIVITY) deterministically.

exercises.title: Verify interactions with Spy and Verify Call
exercises.task: A flow should call the audit logger exactly once and must enrich the event before the HTTP request runs. Write MUnit assertions that (a) verify the audit processor was called once, and (b) spy the event just before the HTTP Request to assert a header was set — without replacing either processor.

given.label: Flow under test
given.code:
<flow name="submit">
  <flow-ref name="audit" doc:name="Audit"/>
  <set-variable variableName="traceId" value="#[uuid()]"/>
  <http:request doc:name="Send" .../>
</flow>

steps: Verify Call: assert the 'Audit' flow-ref was invoked times=1
Spy the 'Send' HTTP Request: in the before-block assert vars.traceId is not null
Note the distinction: Mock replaces, Spy observes without replacing, Verify counts

solution.label: submit-test.xml (assertions)
solution.code:
<munit:validation>
  <munit-tools:verify-call processor="flow-ref" times="1">
    <munit-tools:with-attributes>
      <munit-tools:with-attribute attributeName="doc:name" whereValue="Audit"/>
    </munit-tools:with-attributes>
  </munit-tools:verify-call>
</munit:validation>

<munit-tools:spy processor="http:request">
  <munit-tools:with-attributes>
    <munit-tools:with-attribute attributeName="doc:name" whereValue="Send"/>
  </munit-tools:with-attributes>
  <munit-tools:before-call>
    <munit-tools:assert-that expression="#[vars.traceId]"
        is="#[MunitTools::notNullValue()]"/>
  </munit-tools:before-call>
</munit-tools:spy>

exercises.solutionNotes: The most-tested MUnit distinction: Mock When replaces a processor; Spy observes the event immediately before/after a processor that still runs; Verify Call asserts how many times a processor executed (times/atLeast/atMost). Here Verify proves the audit call happened once, and Spy checks state at a point in the flow without altering behaviour.

exercises.title: Gate the build on MUnit coverage
exercises.task: CI must fail the build when MUnit coverage drops below thresholds. Configure the munit-maven-plugin so coverage runs, enforces 80% application / 70% resource / 60% flow coverage, and breaks mvn test when unmet.

given.label: Given
given.code:
- Tests live in src/test/munit and pass
- Pipeline runs 'mvn clean test'
- Requirement: fail the build below the thresholds above

steps: Add the coverage block to the munit-maven-plugin configuration
Set runCoverage=true and failBuild=true
Set requiredApplicationCoverage / requiredResourceCoverage / requiredFlowCoverage
Confirm mvn test fails when a flow is under-covered
exercises.solutionNotes: MUnit binds to the Maven test phase; the coverage block with failBuild=true turns coverage into a CI quality gate — the build breaks below the application/resource/flow thresholds. Tests are excluded from the deployable JAR. The typical pipeline is mvn clean package (compile + MUnit + coverage) → publish → mvn deploy -DmuleDeploy per environment, promoting the same artifact.
